lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market U.S. curtain and linen mills providing repeat private-label, contract cut-and-sew, custom institutional, or made-to-specification production to external retailers, hospitality operators, healthcare customers, designers, and distributors.
lens.excluded_activities: Stock or owned-brand household textile manufacturing without a recurring outsourced-service relationship; direct-to-consumer retail; wholesalers; window-treatment installation; commercial laundering or linen rental; captive production units; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers place recurring purchase orders or production programs for sheets, towels, curtains, draperies, table linens, shower curtains, and related household textiles, priced per unit, lot, style, or contract specification rather than by subscription.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is narrowed to private-label, contract, and made-to-specification production because NAICS 314120 primarily covers goods manufacturing, including stock and custom products for retail sale, while recurring outsourced production has materially different eligibility and commercial-retention economics.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader 3141 Textile Furnishings Mills occupation mix rather than wage-weighted task exposure for 314120. Sewing-machine operators are the largest occupation, and winding, weaving, material-moving, maintenance, inspection, shipping, and supervision are also prominent. OECD finds high-skill cognitive occupations more exposed to software AI and lists textile pressers among the least exposed, physical occupations. I assign high exposure to specification intake, quoting, production planning, procurement, customer service, documentation, and some visual inspection, but low exposure to embodied sewing, cutting, handling, maintenance, and tactile quality work. The broad-industry occupation mix may understate office/design content in custom linen programs or overstate exposure if small mills are especially manual.
a.rationale: The qualifying workflow contains useful digital tasks around customer specifications, style and material variants, quoting, scheduling, procurement, order status, and quality records. Yet the core conversion of purchased fabric into finished goods remains hands-on and machine-bound, so the exposed wage-weighted share is limited.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 314120 occupational-and-wage special tabulation, combined with task diaries from private-label curtain and linen plants and observed current automation coverage, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: OEWS evidence combines carpet/rug mills with curtain/linen mills at NAICS 3141.
Employment counts are not wage weights and do not measure within-occupation task exposure.
Software-AI exposure omits conventional sewing automation and may omit AI embedded in robotics.

rho.rationale: Within five years, small contract manufacturers can implement AI in document intake, quote preparation, purchasing, scheduling, customer communications, pattern/specification checks, and quality reporting. Conversion is constrained by fragmented software, short runs, changing fabrics, physical samples, retailer compliance, false-positive costs, and limited engineering capacity.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor-neutral case studies from U.S. home-textile contract plants reporting pre/post labor hours, error rates, schedule adherence, sample cycles, and realized headcount or avoided hiring would materially move the range.
caveats: No 314120-specific survey measures production use of AI rather than generic digitization.
The estimate assumes usable digital order and specification data.
Benefits from ordinary sewing machinery are excluded unless AI is essential to the workflow.

e.rationale: Census defines these establishments as manufacturers of household textile products from purchased materials and explicitly includes both stock and custom production for retail sale. Repeat private-label and institutional contract programs plausibly qualify as outsourced production, but many firms sell their own stock products or operate as product brands rather than service providers. Eligibility is therefore a minority of the frozen LMM population.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level roster of the 36 dataset-estimated LMM firms, coded for percentage of revenue from repeat private-label, contract, or institutional made-to-spec work and for standalone ownership, would directly establish eligibility.
caveats: The frozen firm count is a margin-bridged estimate rather than an observed list.
Custom products sold to individual retail customers are inside NAICS but generally outside the external-business service lens.
Public information often does not distinguish private-label manufacturing from branded wholesale revenue.

s5.bridge: Gallup's fall-2024 U.S. survey reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and that 52.3% of employer businesses were owned by people age 55 or older. I map that employer-business intention rate to eligible LMM contract curtain/linen mills, with a modest downward adjustment for completion risk, customer-transfer risk, equipment financing, and the distinction between any transfer and a qualifying control transfer. This may overstate realized transfers because plans can lapse, or understate unannounced strategic and family transactions.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership supports meaningful transfer probability, while recurring private-label programs and production teams make eligible firms more transferable than owner-personal craft shops. Buyer concentration and imported-product pressure reduce the number that will complete a viable control transaction.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year 314120 deal census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, trade advisers, and state business registries, matched to ownership age and control outcome, would replace the general-employer proxy.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses rather than manufacturing, LMM firms, or this NAICS.
Stated sale, gift, or public-offering plans are not completed qualifying control transfers.
A small eligible denominator makes realized five-year rates volatile.

q.rationale: Per-unit and program pricing lets a contract manufacturer initially retain workflow savings, but large retailers, hospitality buyers, and distributors can rebid, benchmark imports, demand cost reductions, or alter sourcing. Savings tied to faster sampling, better compliance, fewer defects, and reliable short runs are more defensible than pure clerical labor reduction.
q.change_evidence: Representative private-label master agreements, bid sheets, annual cost-down clauses, open-book provisions, renewal cadence, customer concentration, and gross-margin histories before and after automation would materially change retention.
caveats: No direct study measures AI-benefit pass-through in U.S. curtain and linen contracts.
The 2021 trade evidence is broad and pandemic-affected, but it establishes strong import benchmarking pressure.
Retention differs materially between retailer private label, custom hospitality work, and small designer programs.

d5.bridge: BLS real sectoral output for the broader 3141 Textile Furnishings Mills industry declined from 90.993 in 2021 to 72.715 in 2025. USITC also reports that home-furnishing imports reached $15.4 billion in 2021 after 33.0% growth, indicating substantial foreign supply, although that year reflected pandemic home-goods demand. I use the broader real-output decline as the direction for domestic contract-manufacturing quantity, moderated by recurring replacement, hospitality, healthcare, and customization needs. The proxy may overstate decline if carpet mills drove the 3141 fall, or understate it if imported curtains and linens displace domestic output faster.
d5.rationale: Households and institutions continue to replace sheets, towels, curtains, and related textiles, but U.S. production faces sustained import competition and the broader group's real output has contracted sharply. The central case is continued, slower contraction rather than disappearance.
d5.change_evidence: Six-digit real shipment volumes for 314120, split between domestic private-label, institutional/custom, and branded retail production, plus five-year customer sourcing plans from major retailers, hotel groups, and healthcare textile buyers, would materially refine the range.
caveats: The output series is NAICS 3141 and includes carpet and rug mills.
USITC home-furnishing imports cover a broader product basket and 2021 was pandemic-distorted.
The narrowed contract-service subset may benefit from reshoring or short-lead-time needs even while total domestic output falls.

o.rationale: Year-five demand that remains for made-to-spec curtains and linens still requires an accountable operator to source fabric, cut, sew, finish, inspect, package, certify, and deliver physical products. Software can automate coordination and parts of inspection, but cannot supply the finished textile without an operating manufacturer. The residual risk is customer insourcing, direct offshore sourcing, or highly automated standardized production.
o.change_evidence: Procurement interviews with private-label retailers, hospitality groups, healthcare systems, and designers about direct sourcing and insourcing, plus automation roadmaps from contract plants, would test the operator-required share.
caveats: This estimate conditions on quantity already remaining in d5 and does not count import substitution twice.
Standard products are more vulnerable to direct offshore procurement than short-run customized programs.
No direct survey measures software-only or self-service displacement for contract home-textile production.

narrative.executive_view: The relevant opportunity is a minority subset of curtain and linen mills that repeatedly manufacture private-label or made-to-spec products for external business customers. These firms have AI-addressable specification, quoting, planning, procurement, and quality workflows, but manual sewing dominates and domestic real output is declining.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can structure customer specifications, accelerate quotes and samples, improve material planning and schedules, automate order-status communication, and flag visual defects or documentation exceptions. It does not remove the need for cutting, sewing, handling, maintenance, tactile checks, and final accountability.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed per-unit or per-program pricing can preserve early savings. Retention erodes when concentrated buyers rebid work, require annual cost-downs, compare offshore quotes, or capture transparent clerical savings; value tied to turnaround, compliance, and low defect rates is more durable.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen dataset estimates 36 firms in the LMM EBITDA band, but the NAICS definition includes stock and custom product manufacturers broadly. Only an unobserved minority is likely to earn enough revenue from repeat outsourced private-label or institutional production to qualify.
narrative.demand_durability: Replacement and institutional consumption keep the physical product basket necessary, while hospitality and healthcare customization can favor responsive suppliers. Still, broader textile-furnishings real output fell each year from 2021 through 2025, and low-cost imported home furnishings set a demanding price benchmark.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The central diligence risk is finding that few of the 36 estimated firms actually fit the service lens. Other risks include offshore substitution, retailer concentration, volatile tariffs and input costs, weak domestic volume, legacy systems, limited AI implementation staff, and benefit pass-through at annual sourcing events.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring specification-heavy private-label production creates repeat digital workflows around a physical product that still needs an accountable operator.
narrative.principal_weakness: Eligibility is unobserved and likely limited, while domestic textile-furnishings output has contracted under strong import pressure.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Form MC-31411: Curtain and Linen Mills
sources.supports: Identifies 314120 establishments as curtain and linen mills manufacturing household textile products from purchased fabric, on a stock or custom basis, and lists sheets, towels, pillows, shower curtains, and related products.

sources.title: BLS OEWS Data Tables for Industry Charts, May 2024
sources.supports: Reports the largest occupations in Textile Furnishings Mills, led by 5,870 sewing machine operators, followed by winding, weaving, production supervision, material moving, maintenance, inspection, shipping, and cutting occupations.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence and the Changing Demand for Skills in the Labour Market
sources.supports: Finds cognitive high-skill occupations more exposed to software AI, non-routine physical occupations less exposed, and textile pressers among the least exposed occupations; also cautions that exposure is not demonstrated adoption.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Gallup's fall-2024 U.S. survey of 1,264 working business owners reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and that 52.3% of employer businesses had owners age 55 or older.

sources.title: Real Sectoral Output for Textile Furnishings Mills (NAICS 3141)
sources.supports: BLS real sectoral output index reported 90.993 in 2021, 86.044 in 2022, 81.916 in 2023, 76.026 in 2024, and 72.715 in 2025.

sources.title: USITC Trade Shifts 2021: Textiles and Apparel
sources.supports: Reports $15.4 billion of U.S. home-furnishing imports in 2021, up 33.0%, and identifies China and India as major textile and home-furnishing suppliers; supports import competition and demand caveats.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Quality Control Inspectors
sources.supports: Explains that advanced vision systems and scanners automate some inspection tasks, while random checks, validation, and texture or performance testing continue to require inspectors.
